manual:
Textes Relatifs A L'Histoire (1056-1081)
XLIX
1056-1081 et 1091, décembre.
name: Saint Peter at Oudenburg, 1061
description:
In the subsequent period, when the buildings of the temple were being constructed on the northern side, the bitumen craftsman (bituminis artifex) (1) was working with his team on the southern side. Now on a certain day, a boy (2) who was handing him stones and cement, placed so heavy a weight of stones on the structure in which they were standing that the structure could not bear the force of such a mass; but, with the entire structure broken, the stones and cement fell with a great noise to the ground. 

(1) Bituminis artifex would designate the master worker or cement specialist who, in this cold and humid northern region, was required to coat the masonry with a cement mixture, no doubt highly cohesive and intended to prevent the disastrous effects of moisture in construction. The chronicler considers it necessary to insist on this point, probably because masonry techniques at that time were all the more noteworthy inasmuch as methods of timber-frame construction were still very widely employed in this northern region.
(2) At this period, we scarcely find any mention in the texts of an assistant mason; this example should be noted for the eleventh century.
source: Victor Mortet: Recueil de textes relatifs à l'histoire de l'architecture et à la condition des architectes en France au Moyen Age. Paris. 1911
originalText:
Subséquente autem tempore, cum aedestempli aedificabantur in septentrionali climalte, bituminis artifex (1) cum suis in meridiano latere operabatur. Quadam vero die, puer (2) qui ei lapides et cementum porrig'ebat, tam grave pondus lapidum machinamento, in quo stabant, superposuit ut vim tantae molis ferre non prevaleret, sed, fracto omni machinamento, lapides et cementum cum magno sonitu super terram corruerunt.

(1) Bituminis artifex désignerait le maître ouvrier cimeuteur qui, dans cette région froide et humide du Nord, devait revêtir la maçonnerie d'un mélange de ciment, sans doute très agglutiné et destiné à prévenir les effets désastreux de Ihumidité dans la construction. Le chroniqueur croit devoir insister sur ce fait, vraisemblablement parce que les procédés de maçonnerie étaient alors d'autant plus à remarquer que ceux de la construction en charpente de bois étaient encore très employés dans cette région du Nord.
(2) Nous n'avons guère à cette époque dans les textes de mention d'aidemaçon; cet exemple est à retenir pour le xi° siècle.
commentOnText: Mortet interprets the term bituminis artifex as a master worker or specialised craftsmen who was required in cold and humid northern regions to coat the masonry with a cement mixture to protect construction from moisture. It can also be a mason who is rained in the art of using tarpitch. The use of natural bitumen is unlikely.

name: Cennini, 1400 – Waterproofing with pitch
description: For this same purpose: first take some of this ship pitch, and apply it and smear the wall with it, boiling hot. name: Pomponius Gauricus, 1504 – Stoneworking
description:
Stone-working (Scalpello) is furthermore carried out with chisel, file, and drill; for the final smoothing, clear water and pumice stone are used. 
For bonding, however, immediately take—should any occasion arise—mastic (masticem) that has been chewed and made pliable like wax, or proceed as follows: mix pitch (picem), Armenian blue (Bergblau, armenium), and wax (ceram), and apply them to the heated joining surfaces.
source: Heinrich Brockhaus: Pomponius Gauricus, De Sculptura, Florence (1504). Leipzig.1886

Zum Anleimen aber nimm sofort bei etwaigen Vorkommnissen gekauten und wie Wachs geschmeidig gemachten Mastix (masticem) oder mache es so: mische Pech (picem), Bergblau (armenium) und Wachs (ceram) und bringe sie auf die erhitzen Verbindungsflächen.

manual: letter to the court of Duke Albrecht V. of Bavaria regarding the delivery of dismantled sculptures
name: Castellini, 1567 – Enclosed mestura
description:
"(...) as if in the arms of the large mercury, make the pegs out of metal and heat them up. and both parts of the marble to be joined together, but not so much that the marble breaks, only enough that it is difficult to hold your hand on top of it: and at the same time breaking up the mixture that is inside one of the boxes and placing it on the fire in a pot, so that it melts slowly little by little, stirring the mixture all the time so that it does not burn or stick to the pot. Once all the above has been done, and the mixture has been heated at the same time, the pegs and both parts of the marble to be joined together must be placed in the holes, and on the part to be joined, so that it will hold together wonderfully, such that the joined parts cannot be shaken in any way.  
And if a finger or some similar part of the statues breaks off, there is nothing better than to heat the parts to be joined together as above, and while they are hot, put a little of the wax from the votes that stick to the chisels between them, because pressing the broken parts together will consolidate them very well.  
This is the advice for the antiquarian and the sculptor."
source: Ellen Weski; Heike Frisien-Leizen (Hrsg.): Das Antiquarium der Münchner Residenz- Katalog der Skulpturen. München, Hirmer Verlag. 1987
originalTitle: Castellinj verzeichnus der letzt gesendten antiquiteten.
originalText:
"(…) ...comealle braccia del mercurio grande overo fatte dette cavicchie di metallo, et riscaldate dette cavicchie, et tutte due le parti di marmo, che s'hanno da congiongere insieme, non pero di maniera, che venesse a spezarsi il marmo, ma solamente tanto, che a fatica se li possa suffrire de tenere la mano sopra: et in un medesimo tempo spezando in pezzi la mistura, che e dentro ad una delle casse, et mettendola al fuoco in una pignatta, si che si disfaccia lentamente a poco a poco, rimenando tutta via detta mistura, accioche non si abrugi, et non s'atachi alla pignatta, cosi disposte tutte le predette cose, et riscaldata in un medemo tempo la mistura, le cavicchie, et tutte due le parti del marmo, che s'hanno da congiungere insieme bisogna mettere dette cavicchie et mistura in detti busi, et per la parte da congiungersi, che cosi fara mirabile presa, talche le parti congionte non potranno squassarsi in modo alcuno.
Et quando sfozasse un dito o qualche simil parte sitile di dette statue, non è cosa megliore che di far riscaldare, come di sopra, le parti che s'hanno a congiongere insieme, et cosi calde, metterle in meggio un poco di quelle cera delli voti, che s'attachano nelle chiose (?), perche stringendo insieme le parti scomesse, viene a consolidare molto bene.
Et tanto si avisa per conseglio dell'antiquario et del scultore."
notes: transcript taken from Ellen Weski; Heike Frisien-Leizen (Hrsg.): Das Antiquarium der Münchner Residenz- Katalog der Skulpturen. München, Hirmer Verlag. 1987

name: Borghini, 1584 – Two types of stucco
description:
Truly, you have said much (Michelozzo continued), and I am almost satisfied, and I will be completely satisfied when you have shown me how to repair an arm or other limb that has been broken off a figure, or how to attach a head or legs to an ancient torso, and how to give new marble the colour of old marble, so that it conforms to the original.

There are two types of stucco used to repair limbs, replied Sirigatto (1).
To make the first, take three pounds of Greek pitch, six of yellow wax, and four ounces of turpentine;
first melt the Greek pitch and wax in a pot over a fire, then add the turpentine, mixing well; then add marble powder to taste, depending on whether you want the mixture to be more solid or more liquid. Next, heat the pieces of marble to be repaired and apply the putty while still hot, and it will adhere very strongly.
But it should be noted that when repairing arms, legs or heads, it is necessary to insert a copper or bronze pin, and not iron, because rust will cause the marble to expand over time. Once the pin is in place, holding both parts of the marble, the putty is applied as described above.

(1) = See Filippo Baldinucci's ‘Vocabolorio del Disegno’ under ‘Stucco’.
source:
Il riposo di Raffaello Borghini. Edithed by A.M. Biscioni; pref. And additional notes by G.G.Bottari. Firenze. 1730

online

Il riposo di Raffaello Borghini. Volume primo. Dalla Società Tipografica de'Classici Italiani, contrada di c. Margherita, N°1118. 1807
originalText:
Di vero, che voi avete detto assai (seguito il Michelozzo) ed io ne rimango quasi contento, e ne saro del tutto, quando mi avrete mostrato, come si rappicchi braccio o altro membro, che si rompesse a una figura, ovverto come si attacchino testa o gambe a una torso antico: e come si dia al marmo nuovo il colore, acciocche all' antico sia conforme.

Di due sorti si fanno di stucchi, per rappiccare le membra insieme, ripose il Sirigatto (1).
Volendo fare il primo, si piglia tre libbre di pece greca, ance sei di cera gialla, e once quattro di trementina; 
e prima si strugge al fuoco in peztola la pece greca e la cera, e poi vi si mette la trementina, benissimo rimescolando insieme: e poscia vi si aggiugne della polvere di marmo a discrezione, secondoche si vuole la materia piu soda o piu liquida: dopo si scaldano o pezzi del marmo; che s'hanno a rattaccare, e caldo vi si mette sopra lo stucco, e cosi verra a fare fortissima presa.
Ma bisogna avvertire, che avendo a rattaccar braccia, gambe, o teste, fa di mestiero mettervi un perno di rame o di bronzo, e non di ferro, perche la ruggine col tempo allarga il marmo: ed accomodato il pewrno, che prenda ambidue le parti del marmo, si mette poscia lo stucco, come e detto.
(1) = V. il Vocabolorio del Disegno di Filippo Baldinucci alla "v. Stucco"

name: Borghini, 1584 – White putty
description:
(continues from 1584.Bor.01)
But if you wish to make the second putty (which will be very white and good for covering the aforementioned putty, because it is ugly to look at and this covers it and does not allow the sticking to show), take some mastico da denti and put it in your mouth, chewing it a little, if you want the putty to be white, then put it on the fire, add a little white wax and very fine marble powder, and mix well, taking care not to let it catch fire, so that the mixture does not turn yellow. Then heat the marble pieces and, while still hot, place them on top, so that they will stick well, leaving them to dry by themselves.
source: Il riposo di Raffaello Borghini. Edithed by A.M. Biscioni; pref. And additional notes by G.G.Bottari. Firenze. 1730
originalText: Ma piacendovi di fare il secondo stucco (il quale sara molto bianco, e buono a dare sopra il convento delle stucco sopradetto, perche e brutto a vedere, e questo il copre, e non lascia apparire l'appiccatura) prenderete mastico da denti, e quello vi porrete in bocca, masticandolo alquanto, volendo lo stucco sia bianco, e poi lo metterete al fuoco, mettetevi dentro un poco di cera bianca, e polvere di marmo sottilissima, ed incorporate bene insieme, avendo cura non pigli fuomo, accio la materia non ingialli: poscia scaldate le parti del marmo, e cosi caldo ponetevelo sopra, che fara buona presa, lasciandolo seccare da se stesso

name: Riccio, 1597 – How to make putty to stick stones together
description: To make good putty, take several ingredients, such as yellow wax, Greek pitch, turpentine, mastic, sifted marble powder and sifted glass sand, place them in a small cauldron and boil everything together. When well made, this putty will last a long time. Here is how to make it. First, take a small copper pot and put in a pound of Greek pitch; second, two ounces of yellow wax; third, a pound and a half of sifted marble powder; fourth, half an ounce of mastic; fifth, half an ounce of pine turpentine, if you do not want to use mastic; and sixth, turpentine. This is an example of how to make stucco, but it cannot be adjusted in this way: ‘Practice makes perfect’, say the master craftsmen.
source: (only online)
originalTitle: XCVIII. MODO DI FAR STUCCO PER ATTACCAR PIETRE INSIEME
originalText: A far buono stucco si piglia più cose, come cera gialla, pece greca, trementina, mastice, polvere di marmo vagliata e rena da bicchieri vagliata, in un calderotto, e si fa bollire ogni cosa insieme, e detto stucco basta assai tempo quando è fatto bene. Qui metto il modo, di farlo. In prima si piglia un calderotto di rame e mettici una libbra di pece greca; secondo, due once di cera gialla; terzo, una libbra e mezzo di polvere di marmo stacciata, quarto, una mezza oncia di mastice; quinto, una mezza oncia di ragia di pino, se non ci vuoi mettere il mastice; e sesto, la trementina. Si è dato un esempio di far lo stucco, ma non si può così aggiustare punto: “Il fare insegna a fare”, dicono i maestri pratichi.
notes: The transcription available on meofonte.it  is based on the edition A. Del Riccio, Istoria delle pietre, a cura di R. Gnoli e A. Sironi, Torino Allemandi, 1996, which in turn is based on the facsimile edition A. Del Riccio, Istoria delle pietre, riproduzione anastatica del cod. 230 della Biblioteca Riccardiana, a cura di P. Barocchi, Firenze S.P.E.S., 1979.

name: Stone, 1640 – Remedy for a flaw or crack in a piece of marble
description: If there is a flaw or hollow in a piece of marble, take the white of fresh eggs and finely powdered hot or unslaked) lime. Mix them together in a bowl using a spoon, making sure the mixture is not too thick. Once prepared, use a piece of clay to build a barrier around the area with the defect. Then pour the prepared mixture into the enclosed space and gently tap the stone with the handle of a tool. This will cause the liquid mixture to seep into the joint or flaw. Leave it to dry for about two days. Afterwards, the repaired area will be as solid to work on as any other part of the stone.
source: Walter Lewis Spiers: The Note-Book and Account Book of Nicholas Stone. Published on the seventh Volume of the Walpole Socciety. Oxford. 1919

a remedy for a flaw or crake in a peece of marble

name: Stone, 1640 – Another way to join marble
description:
Another way to join marble so that it endures heat:
Take a prouaturo, first wash it well in warm water to remove the greasiness, then grind it very finely on a painter’s stone. Mix it with the finest powder of slaked lime. This holds so firmly that the marble will break more easily elsewhere than at the joint.
source: Walter Lewis Spiers: The Note-Book and Account Book of Nicholas Stone. Published on the seventh Volume of the Walpole Socciety. Oxford. 1919

name: Boselli, 1650 – How to make the mixture for attaching

ingredients.ingredient: greek pitch from Cassa [pece gregu da Cassa]

description:
First, before proceeding further I want to say how one makes the mixture which we use in restoring antique statues, without which one labors in vain. 

Into a basin or little copper pot into which nothing else has been put previously, you put two libre of greek pitch from Cassa (pece gregu da Cassa), the clearest and cleanest that you can find, and less than 1 ounce of yellow wax (Cera Gialla), and without breaking it up you melt everything together; then you make a finely ground powder from chips of clean, white marble, and little by little you add it into the basin of liquefied rosin and wax, and with a wooden paddle you constantly stir and remix until the point when, raising the stick, the material runs thinly in the same place. The proof that it is good will be that if you drop a little onto a cold slab, and leave it alone until it is completely cool, then take it and try to break it; if it breaks it is made; if it does not break, add more powder until it does break: with this mixture I can attach everything. 

But first I will warn that you should heat well both pieces which are to be attached together, and they should be heated well enough so that when you place your hand or finger on it to test, you can only bear to touch the heated parts for an instant; then pour the mixture over it, and attach the pieces together, clean off the excess mixture clinging to the outside, until you are certain that the pieces are in their proper places according to how you previously countersigned them. 
I warn that the pieces should rather be too hot (p.91) than not hot enough, and that in working them, the chisels should always remove stone favoring the joins and with care for not detaching the pieces, and not bursting the join.
source: Translation of the Boselli text on the Restoration of Antique Sculpture, Osseruationi della Scoltura Antica, Book V, fols. 171v-176v. by Phoebe Dent Weil (1967): Contributions toward a History of Techniques: I. Orfeo Boselli on the of Antique Sculpture. Studies in Conservation. Vol. 12. No. 3. Pp. 81-101
originalTitle:
(173ᵛ) MODO DA FAR LA MISTURA PER ATTACHARE
CAP XVII
originalText:
Prima di passare auuanti uoglio dire come si faccia la mistura che adoperamo in risaturare le statue antiche, senza la quale in uano si faticharebbe. 
Metterai in una pila, o uasetto di Rame doue non sia stata altra cosa, libre doi di pece gregu da Cassa, la più chiara, e polita, che tu troui et meno di mezza oncia di Cera, Gialla, et a foco senza fiara farai liquefare ogni cosa assieme; poi hauerai poluere fatta di scaglie di marmo bianco polita, e ben macinata, et appoco a poco la ponerai dentro detta pila, e pece e Cera liquefatta, con una stecha fatta a Cuchiarella sempre manigiando, e rimesco- lando, sin tanto, che alzando detta stecha la materia corra sottilmente nel istesso sito. La proua se sia buona sarà che mandandone un pezo sopra una lastra fredda, e lasciatala stare sino che sia rifredat affatto, poi presa e forzata a rompersi, se si spezza è fatta, se non si spezza, ui aggiungerai poluere, tanto che si spezzi: con questa potrai attachar ogni cosa. 
Ma prima auuertirai scaldare bene l’uno, et l’altro pezo da attaccarsi insieme, et scaldato a sufficientza sarà, quando postaui la mano, o dita per attastare, poco tempo possi soffrire il calore de’ detti pezzi scaldati: indi buttataui sopra la mistura, et postisi insieme, nettiarai fuori della mistura colata, sino che siue certificato che sono bene attaccati i pezzi, et per prima cautione troppo caldi, che meno, o poco, e che nel lauorarli i ferri sempre leuino pietra a fauore della attachatura, e con diligenza per non stachare i pezzi, e non schiantare le Comissure.
notes: the exact date of publication is unknown, approximately around 1650
commentOnText:
description of how to add the right amount of filler;
description of how to gently(!) warm the stone surfaces

manual: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society
name: Royal Society, 1673 – A kind of plaster
description: To try , whether such a kind of Plaister may not be made by taking powder of Gypsum so prepared, as is the artificial Marble abovementioned, and mixing with it a certain proportion of Rosin, Colophon or Pitch, as also Brimstone and crude Shells, all beaten to dust; adding thereto some water, and heating all upon the fire, and then using it hot; it being perhaps not amiss also, to add some wax and oyl of Terebinthin, as being very binding, and ingredients in some Cements.
source: Directions for inquiries concerning stones and other materials for the use of building; together with a suggestion for retriving the art of hardning and tempering steel for cutting porphyre and other hard marbles. (1673). Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London, 8(93), 6010–6015. https://doi.org/10.1098/rstl.1673.0010 (Original work published April 21, 1673)
notes: author unknown
commentOnText: The author is trying to find an explanation of 'how the plaster observable today in venice could have been done'. He is using  his observations of 'artifical marble' (scagliogla), made mostly from plaster of Paris and combines it with his material knowledge of weatherproof 'cements' on (t)his existing material knowledge.

name: Kunckel, 1679 – An excellent glue
description:
ake 4 loth of good glue, and let it soak overnight in distilled vinegar.
On the following day, let it briefly simmer in the vinegar.
Then take a garlic bulb, crush and grind it in a mortar, and add 1 loth of ox gall to it. This will become a kind of juice. Strain this juice through a linen or hair cloth into the warm glue.

Next, take mastix (mastic) and sarcocollae, half a quintlein of each; then sandarac and turpentine, one quintlein of each. Grind the sandarac and mastix finely, and place them together with the animal glue (or the gum sarcocollae) and the turpentine into a small glass vessel. Pour over it one ounce of the strongest brandy, seal it well, and let it stand for 3 hours over gentle heat, shaking the glass a few times in between. Then pour everything into the warm glue, and beat it continuously with a wooden stick until some of the moisture has evaporated and the glue has cooled.
When you wish to use it, take as much or as little as needed, soften it in a bit of sharp vinegar until it melts over coals, and use it as described above in the section mentioned.

To glue stones, one may add a bit of Tripel (also known as drippel, a fine polishing chalk) or finely ground chalk.

For glass, one may also add to the mixture a small amount of finely ground Venetian glass alongside a little Tripel. If you wish to use it on brass, copper, or iron, you may add filings of the same kind of metal, ground as finely as possible. You may also mix in some Hausen-Blasen (fish glue).

If this glue is to hold even better in water, one must infuse it with strong printer’s varnish (Drucker-Furniß).
However, one should only prepare as much varnish-glue mixture as needed for immediate use, since it cannot be softened again afterward
source: Johannes Kunckel: Ars Vitraria Experimentalis (…). Franfurt/Leipzig/Jena. 1679
originalTitle: Ein trefflicher Leim / damit man Holz / Steine / Gläser / ja auch Metall an einander befestigen kan.
commentOnText: first mentioning of brandy ('Branntwein') as alcoholic solvent as well as vinegar (liquefier)

name: Kunckel, 1679 – A good stone glue or grotto cement
description:
Take two parts resin, melt it, and skim off the foam. Then add four parts yellow wax.
When it has fully melted together, take stone powder (stone dust) from the very stone you wish to glue or cement—two or three parts, or as much as needed so that the glue takes on the color of the stone. To this, add one part powdered sulfur.
Incorporate everything together—first over gentle heat, then finally knead it by hand in warm water. This will hold very well.
However, the stones to be glued must be thoroughly dry and made fairly hot beforehand,
so that the cement or glue may bond with them more effectively.
source: Johannes Kunckel: Ars Vitraria Experimentalis (…). Franfurt/Leipzig/Jena. 1679
originalText: Nimm 2 Theil Harz / laß es schmelzen / und sondere den Schaum davon ab; thue darunter 4. Theil gelb Wachs / wanns zusammen wohl geschmolzen / so nimm Steinmehl / von den Stein den du kütten oder leimen wilt / 2. oder 3. Theil / oder so viel / daß der Leim die FArbe des Steins bekomme; thue dazu ein Theil gepulverten Schwefel / incorporire alles zusammen / erstlich über gelinden Feuer / und letzlich mit den Händen in warmen Wasser / so hälts sehr wohl / es müssen aber die Steine / die geleimet werden sollen / wohl trocken und ziemlich heiß zuvor gemacht werden / damit sich die Kütte oder Leim besser damit vereinigen können.
commentOnText: first mentioning to "take off foam" when melting resin

name: Baldinucci, 1681 – White stucco for matching surfaces
description: A white stucco used to fill joints or seams [commettiture] of upright statues. It is a mixture of tooth mastic [mastico da denti], chewed and melted over fire with white wax [cera bianca] and finely ground marble powder [polvere di marmo sottile].
source: Filippo Baldinucci: Vocabolario toscano dell' arte del disegno : nel quale si esplicano i propri termini e voci, non solo della pittura, scultura & architettura, ma ancora di altre arti a quelle subordinate, e che abbiano per fondamento il disegno : con la notizia de'nomi e qualità delle gioie, metalli, pietre dure, marmi, pietre tenere, sassi, legnami, colori, strumenti, ed ogn'altra materia, che seruir possa, tanto alla costruzione di edificj e loro ornato, quanto alla stessa pittura e scultura. Firenze. 1681
originalText: Uno stucco col quale si turano i convenenti, o commettiture delle statue rette. È una mestura di mastico da denti, masticato e fuso al fuoco con cera bianca, e polvere di marmo sottile.
furtherBibliography: Translation of the Boselli text on the Restoration of Antique Sculpture, Osseruationi della Scoltura Antica, Book V, fols. 171v-176v. by Phoebe Dent Weil (1967): Contributions toward a History of Techniques: I. name: Baldinucci, 1681 – Stucco for chiselling
description: A compound made of Greek pitch [pece greca], yellow wax [cera gialla], and crushed brick [matton pesto], used to fill objects that are to be chased (engraved in relief).
source: Filippo Baldinucci: Vocabolario toscano dell' arte del disegno : nel quale si esplicano i propri termini e voci, non solo della pittura, scultura & architettura, ma ancora di altre arti a quelle subordinate, e che abbiano per fondamento il disegno : con la notizia de'nomi e qualità delle gioie, metalli, pietre dure, marmi, pietre tenere, sassi, legnami, colori, strumenti, ed ogn'altra materia, che seruir possa, tanto alla costruzione di edificj e loro ornato, quanto alla stessa pittura e scultura. Firenze. 1681
originalText: Un composto di pece greca, cera gialla, e matton pesto, con che si riempiono quelle cose, che debbon esser cesellate.
furtherBibliography: Translation of the Boselli text on the Restoration of Antique Sculpture, Osseruationi della Scoltura Antica, Book V, fols. 171v-176v. by Phoebe Dent Weil (1967): Contributions toward a History of Techniques: I. Orfeo Boselli on the of Antique Sculpture. Studies in Conservation. Vol. 12. No. 3. Pp. 81-101
commentOnText:
the mass is meant to be chisseled/engrade - not an adhesive but more a firm plaster;

name: Baldinucci, 1681 – Stucco for reassembling or repairing statues
description: A mixture of Greek pitch (pece greca), yellow wax (cera gialla), and turpentine (trementina) with marble powder (polvere di marmo), used to reattach the broken parts of statues. The pieces are first internally fixed with bronze or copper pins (not iron), because iron rusts over time and causes the holes in the marble to expand.
source: Filippo Baldinucci: Vocabolario toscano dell' arte del disegno : nel quale si esplicano i propri termini e voci, non solo della pittura, scultura & architettura, ma ancora di altre arti a quelle subordinate, e che abbiano per fondamento il disegno : con la notizia de'nomi e qualità delle gioie, metalli, pietre dure, marmi, pietre tenere, sassi, legnami, colori, strumenti, ed ogn'altra materia, che seruir possa, tanto alla costruzione di edificj e loro ornato, quanto alla stessa pittura e scultura. Firenze. 1681
originalTitle: Stucco da ricommettere o acconciare statue.
originalText: Una mestura di pece greca, cera gialla, e trementina con polvere di marmo, con la quale si ricongiungono i pezzi delle statue rotte, impernando prima interiormente i pezzi con perni di bronzo o di rame, e non di ferro; perché la ruggine di esso col tempo dilata i fori ne’ marmi.
furtherBibliography: Translation of the Boselli text on the Restoration of Antique Sculpture, Osseruationi della Scoltura Antica, Book V, fols. 171v-176v. by Phoebe Dent Weil (1967): Contributions toward a History of Techniques: I. Orfeo Boselli on the of Antique Sculpture. Studies in Conservation. Vol. 12. No. 3. Pp. 81-101

name: Neve, 1703 – To make hot cement
description:
Take one Pound of Rosin, and a Quarter of a Pound of Bees-wax, halt an Ounce of fine Brickdust, half an Ounce of Chalkdust, or Powder of Chalk ; sift both the Brickdust and Chalk-dust thro' a fine Hair Sieve (you may beat the Brick and the Chalk in a Mortar, before you sift it) boil all together in a Pipkin, about a quarter of an Hour, stirring it all the while with an Iron, or a Piece of Lath, or such like; then take it off, and let it stand 4 or five Minutes, and it is fit for Use. 
Note, The Bricks to be cemented with this kind of Cement, must be made hot by the Fire before you spread the Cement on them, and then rub them to and fro, one upon another, as Joiners do, when they glew two Boards together.
source: Richard Neve: The city and country purchaser's and builder's dictionary: or, the complete builder's guide. ... Originally written and compiled by Richard Neve, Philomath. The third edition, corrected and improved throughout. 1736
originalText: same as Mox_1703_02
notes: recipes taken from the digitalized version of 1703
commentOnText: takes the chapter about cememt word-for-word from Moxon 1703

name: Moxon, 1703 – Bricklayers work
description:
Moreover there is other Morter used in making of Water-courses, Cisterns, Fish-ponds, etc. which is very hard and durable, as may be seen at Rome, at this day, which is called Maltha from a kind of Bitumen Dug there;  for as they build most firm Walls thereof naturally, so they use it in making of Cisterns to hold Water, and all manner of Water-works  and also in finishing or Plastering of Fronts to represent Stone.

And I find two kinds of Artifices used by the Antients, both of which is compounded of Lime and Hogs-grease, but to one is added the Juice of Figs, and to the other Liquid Pitch, and the Lumps of Lime are first wet or flack’d with Wine, then pounded or beat with Hogs-grease and juice of Figs or with the same Pitch; that which hath Pitch in it, is blacker and  easily distinguished from the other by its Colour, and that which is Plastered with this Tarrace is done over with Linseed Oil.
source: Joseph Moxon: Mechanick exercises, or, The doctrine of handy-works : applied to the arts of smithing, joinery, carpentry, turning, bricklayery : to which is added Mechanick dyalling: shewing how to draw a true sun-dyal on any given plane … Printed for Dan. Midwinter and Tho. Leigh. London. 1703

name: Moxon, 1703 – The art of making two sorts of cements, for the cementing bricks
description: There are two sorts of Cement, which some Bricklayers use in Cementing of Bricks for some kind of Mouldings, or in Cementing a, block of Bricks, as they call it, for the Carving of Scrolls or Capitals or such like, etc. One is called cold Cement, the other is called hot Cement, because the former is made and used without Fire, but the latter is both made and used with Fire; the cold Cement being accounted a Secret, is known but to few Bricklayers, but the hot Cement is common.
source: Joseph Moxon: Mechanick exercises, or, The doctrine of handy-works : applied to the arts of smithing, joinery, carpentry, turning, bricklayery : to which is added Mechanick dyalling: shewing how to draw a true sun-dyal on any given plane … Printed for Dan. Midwinter and Tho. Leigh. London. 1703

name: Moxon, 1703 – To make the cold cement
description: Take half a Pound of Cheshire-Cheese, pair of the Rine, and throw it away, cut or grate the Cheese very small, and put it into a Pot, put to it about a pint of Cows-milk, let it stand all Night, the next Morning get the Whites of 12 or 14 Eggs, then take half a Pound of the best unslackt or Quick Lime that you can get, and beat it to Powder in a Morter, then sift it through a fine Hair Sieve into a Tray or Bole of Wood, or into an Earthen Dish, to which put the Cheese and Milk, and stir them well together with a Trowel, or such like thing breaking the Knots of Cheese, if there be any, then add the Whites of the Eggs, and Temper all welltogether, and so use it; this Cement will be a White Colour, but if you would have it of the Colour of the Brick, put into it either some very fine Brick-Dust, or Almegram, not too much, but only just to colour it.
source: Joseph Moxon: Mechanick exercises, or, The doctrine of handy-works : applied to the arts of smithing, joinery, carpentry, turning, bricklayery : to which is added Mechanick dyalling: shewing how to draw a true sun-dyal on any given plane … Printed for Dan. Midwinter and Tho. Leigh. London. 1703

name: Moxon, 1703 – To make the hot cement
description:
Take one Pound of Rozin, one Quarter of a Pound of Bees-Wax, half an Ounce of fine Brick-Dust, half an Ounce of Chalk-Dust, or Powder of Chalk, sift both the Brick-Duft and Chalk-Dust through a fine Hair Sieve, (you may beat the Brick and the Chalk in a Morter, before you sift it ) boil altogether in a Pipkin, or other Vessel, about a quarter of an hour, stirring it all the while with an Iron or a piece of Lath or such like, then take it of, and let it stand 4 or 5 Minutes, and 'tis fit for use.

Note, That the Bricks that are to be Cemented with this kind of Cement, must be made hot by the Fire before you spread the Cement on them, and then rub them to and fro on one another, as Joiners do, when they Glew two Boards together.
source: Joseph Moxon: Mechanick exercises, or, The doctrine of handy-works : applied to the arts of smithing, joinery, carpentry, turning, bricklayery : to which is added Mechanick dyalling: shewing how to draw a true sun-dyal on any given plane … Printed for Dan. Midwinter and Tho. Leigh. London. 1703
commentOnText: seems to be older than Neves (1703), started in 1677 and was fianlly published in 1703

name: Bluteau, 1716 – Lithocola
description: Lithocôla: Glue, or bitumen, made with marble powder, pitch, and egg whites, used to solder and cement stones together. Gluten, quo lapides ferruminantur.
source: Bluteau, R. (1716). Vocabulario portuguez & latino, Vol. 5. Officina de Pascoal da Sylva. Lisboa
originalText: Lithocôla : Cola, ou betume, que se faz com pò de mármore, pez & claras de ovo, para soldar & conglutinar as pedras. Gluten, quo lapides ferruminantur.
commentOnText: in the 1716 issue of the Vocabolario, Bluteau also gives definition of bitumen as the natural earth-pitch from Judea and Bitumen as the adhesive

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone-putty
description: take chalk, sulfur, glass, resin, every one the same amount, crush them well and mix them together thoroughly
source:
Johann Melchior Cröcker: Der wohl anführende Mahler: welcher curiöse Liebhaber lehret, wie man sich zur Mahlerey zubereiten, mit Oel-Farben umgehen, Gründe, Fürnisse und andere darzu nöthige Sachen verfertigen, die Gemählde geschickt auszieren, vergülden, versilbern, accurat lacquiren, und saubere Kupffer-Stiche ausarbeiten solle. Diesem ist noch beygefüget Ein Kunst-Cabinet rarer und geheim gehaltener Erfindungen.  Alles aus eigener Erfahrung aufgezeichnet von Johann Melchior Crökern, Erff. Med. Cand. Jena : Bey Johann Rudolph Crökern. 1736.

online
originalText: Nimm Kreide, Schwefel, Glaß, Harz, jedes gleich viel, zerstosse es wohl, und rühre es wohl untereinander

recipes taken from the digitalized version of 1736, online

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone-putty
description: Take 1 loth of wax, 1½ loths of mastic, 1 quentl. of  lead white, grind everything into powder and mix with wax. For red stones, take goat's blood, unslaked lime and red chalk and mix together.
source: s.a.
originalText: Nimm Wachs 1 Loth, Mastix anderthalb Loth, 1. Quentl. Bleiweiß, mache alles zu Pulver, und vermische es mit Wachse. Zu rothen Steinen, nimme man Bocks-Blut, ungeleschten Kalk, rothe Kreide, und vermischt es.

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone-putty
description: Take purified resin and sulfur, mix well, add pine soot [translation], crushed brick powder, a little glass, unslaked lime and stone dust, mix well and melt together in a pot.
source: s.a.
originalText: Nimm geläutert Harz und Schwefel, mische es wohl, thue Kienrauch darzu, gestossen Ziegel-Mehl, ein wenig Glaß, ungeleschten Kalk und Stein-Staub, vermische es wohl, und laß es in einem Topffe unter einander schmelzen.

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone-putty
description:
Take a quarter pound of sulfur, 3 pounds of stone dust, 8 loth of resin, mix it all together, pour it into cold water, and work it thoroughly for an hour.
When you want to use the putty, the stones must always be warm.
source: s.a.
originalText:
Nimm Schwefel dreh(i?) viertel Pfund, Stein-Staub 3. Pfund, 8. Loth Harz, zerlaß es untereinander, giesse es ins kalte Wasser, und arbeite es darinne eine Stunde wohl durch einander.
Wenn man die Kitte gebrauchen will, müssen die Steine allezeit warm sein.
commentOnText: here the generic term "resin" used, not specified

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone-putty
description: Take equal parts of pitch and resin, one third sulfur, and a little wax, melt everything together, add file shavings [translation] and crushed hammer-waste, stir it all together.
source: s.a.
originalText: Nimm Pech und Harz, jedes gleich viel, Schwefel den dritten Theil, und ein wenig Wachs, zerlasse alles, thue Feil-Späne darein und zerstossenen Hammerschlag, rühre es untereinander.
commentOnText: pitch and resin named separately!

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone-putty
description: Take pitch, resin, sulfur, crushed glass, brickdust, stir all well over the fire
source: s.a.
originalText: Nimm Pech, Harz, Schwefel, gestossen Glaß, Ziegelstein-Mehl, rühre solches über dem Feuer wohl durch.
commentOnText: pitch and resin named separately!

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A good stone-putty
description: Take 12 loth of colophony, one and a half loth of yellow wax, 1 loth of Venetian turpentine, 1/2 loth of crushed mastic, melt everything in a cauldron, then add 2 handfuls of white marble or other hard stones crushed into powder, or just brick powder, and stir continuously. This will form a paste that can be used to fill the joints between the quarter pieces. It is resistant to water, cold and sun.
source: s.a.
originalText: Nimm 12 Loth Colophonien, anderthalb Loth gelb Wachs, 1 Loth Venedischen Terpentin, 1 halb Loth gestossenen Mastix, laß alles in einem Kessel zergehen, alsdenn 2 Hände voll von weissem Marmor oder andern harten Steine gestossenes Mehl, oder nur Ziegel_mehl darein gestreut, und beständig umgerühret, so wird ein Teig daraus, damit kan man die Fugen der Quater-Stücke zusammen kitten, es hält Wasser, Kälte, Sonne aus.
commentOnText: colophony and mastix named separately, in specification to resin in other recipes

name: Cröcker, 1736 – Stone-putty
description: take tree oil [see translation], ox tallow, textile or wool fibres [see translation], crushed glass, unslacked lime, resin. These pieces, when mixed well together, make a good adhesive for stonework.
source: s.a.
originalText: Nimm Baum-Oel, Unschlitt, Scheer-Flocken, gestossen Glaß, ungeleschten Kalk, Harz, diese Stücke unter einander wohl gemischt, giebt einen guten Kitt in Wasser zu Stein-Werk.

name: Cröcker, 1736 – Stone- or wood-putty
description: Take 1 lot wax, 2 lot pitch, 2 lot sulfur, 1 lot brickdust, 1 lot lime, 1 lot oil
source: s.a.
originalText: Nimm 1. Loth Wachs, 2. L. Pech, 2. L. Schwefel, 1. Loth Ziegel-Mehl, 1. Loth Kalk, 1. L. Oel

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone glue
description: Take 1 pound resin, 8 loth of sulfur, pitch as much as an Eh [translation] is big, 2 handfuls of hammer-waste, as much stone dust, as much wax as pitch, put everything together in a pot, let it melt, in another dish, melt the sulphur, pour it into the pot with the other ingredients, and let it stand a little longer over the fire, pour it into cold water and mix it carefully with wet hands.
source: s.a.
originalText: Nimm 1 Pfund Harz, 8 Loth Schwefel, Pech so viel als ein Eh(?) groß ist, Hammerschlag 2 Hände voll, auch so viel Stein-Staub, so viel Wachs als Pech, thue alles zusammen in einen Topf, laß es zergehen, in einem anderen Geschirr aber den Schwefel, laß ihn darinne zergehen, und giesse ihn zu den vorigen Sachen in den Topf, und lass es noch ein wenig mit einander über dem Feuer stehen, schütte es in kalt Wasser und mit den feuchten Händen mische es wohl untereinander

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone glue
description: Take equal amounts of resin and sulfur, melt them over a fire, stir in as much Venetian glass as necessary, and if you want to make glue, warm the stone.

name: Cröcker, 1736 – A stone glue

manual: Chamber's Universal Dictionary of Arts and Sciences
name: Chambers, 1741 – Caement
description:
CAEMENT is particulary used in architecture, for a strong, binding sort of mortar, used to bind, or unite bricks or stones together, for some kinds of mouldings; or to make block of bricks, for the carving of scrolls, capitals, etc.

It is of two sorts: The hot Caement, which is most common, is made of rosin, bees-wax, brick-dust and chalk, boiled together. The bricks to be caemented are heated, and rubbed one upon another, with Caement between
source: Chambers, E. (with Internet Archive). (1741). _Cyclopædia; or, An Universal Dictionary of Arts and Sciences. The fourth (corrected and amended) edition. Vol. I (5. Edition). D. Midwinter; W. Innys; J. & P. Knapton; A. Ward; C. Rivington; E. Symon; S. Birt; D. Browne; T. Longman; R. Hett; C. Hitch; L. Hawes; J. Hodges; J. Shuckburgh; A. Millar; M. Senex; Executors of J. Darby

name: Gilly, 1797 – Nr. 83. A good fire- or hot-putty
description:
A good fire- or hot-putty is prepared as follows: Take 24 loth of colophony or pitch, 3 loth of yellow wax, 2 loth of turpentine, 1 loth of cast mastic, 1 loth of sulphur and a good handful of brick dust.
These ingredients are melted in a pot or cauldron over the fire and stirred thoroughly. If this putty is to be used immediately, the joints must be heated with glowing charcoal or with a hot iron placed upon them, and the mixture is then poured in whilst still red-hot.
This stone putty can therefore only be applied to flat-lying stones, such as in basins, water reservoirs and the like. It hardens immediately, so that the excess must be chiselled away. 
One can make a supply of this fire putty to last for many years, storing it in pieces and, when needed, chipping off and melting as much as is required.
source: Gilly, D. (1797). Handbuch der Land-Bau-Kunst: Vorzüglich in Rücksicht auf die Construction der Wohn- und Wirthschaftsgebäude für angehende Cameral-Baumeister und Oekonomen. Erster Theil. D. G. Friderici. Berlin
originalTitle: Nr.83 - Ein Feuer- oder heißer Kitt
originalText:
Ein guter Feuer- oder heißer Kitt wird folgendermaßen zubereitet: Man nimmt 24 Loth Colophonium oder Pech, 3 Loth gelbes Wachs, 2 Loth Terpentin, 1 Loth gegoßnen Mastix, 1 Loth Schwefel und eine gute Hand voll Ziegelmehl.
Diese Massen werden in einem Topf oder Grapen auf dem Feuer zerlassen und fleißig umgerührt. Wenn dieser Kitt sogleich gebraucht werden soll, so müssen die Fugen mit glühenden Holzkohlen oder mit einem darauf gelegten starken glühenden Eisen, heiß gemacht werden, und so wird die Masse glühend eingegossen.
Man kann daher diesen Steinkitt nur alleine bei plattliegenden Steinen, als bei Bassins, Wasser-Reservoirs u. dergl. anbringen. Er wird gleich hart, so dass der überstehende weggemeißelt werden muss. 
Von diesem Feuerkitt kann man auf viele Jahre Vorrath machen, solchen in Stücken aufheben und bei Gebrauch so viel abschlagen und schmelzen als man benöthiget ist.

manual: Istruzione elementare per gli studiosi della scultura
name: Carradori, 1802 – Article 11. Restoring ancient sculptures
description:
But for greater security, a mixture of greek pitch [pece greca] is made, clear and clean, which is put to simmer on the fire, and in which, when it is thick and boiling, add finely ground marble powder, passed through a silk veil or taffeta sieve, stirring it all at the same time with a wooden stick, to the point of making a thick/dense glue.
This is kept warm, while both parts to be joined together, as well as the pivot, are heated with burning coals. The temperature of these should not be less than the supporting pin. 
Then, pour a little of the warm mixture on a wet slab (of marble) to make two small lumps to be placed inside the holes, where the pivot is to go. Then both the joints of the parts are greased with the same tender mixture, which must first be marked, and then with skill, when they are very hot, they are applied diligently in place, trying to cool the pieces as quickly as possible with water and a sponge, always supporting them in the same place until, when the mixture has set, they can no longer move.
source:
Francesco Carradori: Istruzione elementare per gli studiosi della scultura. Firenze. 1802

Matti Auvinnen: Elementary instructions for students of sculpture by Francesco Carradori. J. Paul Getty Museum. Los Angeles. 2002

online and published
originalTitle:
Articolo XI
Del mondo di restaurare le sculture antiche
originalText:
Ma per sucurezza maggiore si compone una mestura di pece greca, chiara e pulita, che si mette a struggere al fuoco, e nella quale, quando è strutta e bolle, si aggiunage/aggmage(?) della polvere di marmo macinata fine, e passata per setaccio di velo o taffeta di seta, dimenando it tutto nell’istesso tempo con una stecca di legno, sino al grado di farne una densa colla. 
Questa si mantien calda, mentre con carboni accesi si scaldano ambedue le parti che si hanno da comettere insieme, non che il pernio di sostegno. Quindi di lascia sopra una lastra bagnata colare un poco della calda mestura, per farne due pastelletti da porsi dentro ai buchi, nei quali ha da entrare il pernio. Si ungono poi con della stessa tenera mestura ambedue le commessure delle parti, le quali devono in prima esserecontrassegnate, epoi con arte, allorchè sono ben calde, so vanno applicando al posto diligentemente, con procurare di freddare i pezzi piu presto che sia possibilie per via d’acqua con spugna, sostenendoli sempre al posto medesimo, sinchè, rapressa la mestura, non si possano piu muovere.
notes: hot coals for heating
furtherBibliography: Matti Auvinnen: Elementary instructions for students of sculpture by Francesco Carradori. J. Paul Getty Museum. Los Angeles. 2002
commentOnText:
1802 version online,
